Farn Polrrrcar- PRACrrcBs CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-s660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Iuly 16,2002
Diane L. Dillon
Post Office Box 126
St. Helena, CA 94574-0126
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance.
Our File No. I-02-082
Dear Ms. Dillon
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r We are providing you with
informal assistance2 since you are a candidate for an office subject to provisions of the
Act
QUESTIONS
1. What relationship may you maintain with your law firm and still effectively eliminate
any source of income or other economic interest for you in the firm?
2. What relationship may you maintain with your law firm and eliminate any source of
income in the firm's clients?
3. When have you received income attributed to you from your law firm's clients?
4. Will your rental of offrce space on a month-to-month basis from the law firm create an
economic interest for you?
5. Could a conflict of interest arise if a partner or employee of the law firm represents a
client before the board?
1 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 1 09- 1 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
' Oriy formal written advice confers the immunity provided under section 83114(b) and
regulation 18329(b)(7). Informal assistance does not provide immunity. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy
enclosed.)


File No. l-02-082
Page No. 2
6. Could you eliminate your economic interest(s) in your husband's business through a
separate property agreement?
7. Can you reduce your real property interests by exchanging equal interests in real
property with your husband and then entering into a separate property agreement with
him?
CONCLUSIONS
1. As a public official, you would have an economic interest in your firm as a source of
income to you if you receive $500 or more within 12 months preceding the time a
relevant decision is made. Additionally, you would have an economic interest in the firm
if you are a director, officer, partner, trustee, or employee of firm. If you meet this
criteria at the time a relevant decision is being made, you would be unable to eliminate
your economic interest in the firm.
2. Because you have an interest of approximalelyl4%o in your law firm, T4o/o of the
income received by the firm from each client will be attributed to you. You would have
an economic interest in a particular client if the I4Yo income attributed to you from the
client aggregates to $500 or more during the relevant l2 month time period. As with the
law firm, you must evaluate whether you have received income from a particular client
during the 12 month time frame prior to the time the relevant decision is to be made,
relinquishing your partnership interest alone will not eliminate an economic interest in a
client.
3. Because your income includes apro rata share of any income of the firm, you will
have "received" income from a client upon your law firm's receipt of the income.
4. Because the terms "interest in real property'' and "leasehold interest" do not include
the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less, you would not have a
real property interest in office space which you rented on a month to month basis,
provided that you do not receive a discount and are paying the fair market rental value for
the office space.
5. We are unable to answer this question without more information relating to a specific
governmental decision. However, please be aware that you will have a conflict of
interest in a governmental decision if the decision will have a material financial effect on
a client in whom you have an economic interest or another economic interest of yours,
including your law firm. (See enclosed document, "Can I Vote? Conflict-of-Interest
Overview.")
6. Since the definition of "investment" includes an ownership interest owned by a public
official or his or her immediate family (i.e., your husband and dependent children), you
could not eliminate your economic (investment) interest in the business entity owned by
your husband through a separate property agreement if you or he has invested $2,000 or
more in this business entity.


File No. I-02-082
Page No. 3
7. You may not eliminate your interest in real property held by your husband after
exchanging equal interests and entering into a separate property agreement since the
definition of "interest in real property" includes an ownership interest owned by your
immediate family.
FACTS
You are a candidate for the Napa County Board of Supervisors. You are also a
partner in Coombs and Dunlap ,LLP, a law firm with offrces in the cities of Napa and St.
Helena. You have a one-seventhQa%) interest in the limited liability partnership. Your
firm serves a broad range of clients in the Napa Valley including parties appearing before
the board of supervisors. The firm also serves as the contract city attorney for the City of
St. Helena and the Town of Yountville.
You seek advice regarding the following
a) Office Buildings. You and five of the law partners own two office buildings in
downtown Napa. This property is held as a tenancy in common.
b) Personal residence: You own your personal residence located in Napa County
c) Triplex: You own a triplex located in Napa County
d) Recreational property: You and four others own recreational property located in Lake
Tahoe.
e) Real Estate Business. Your husband is the sole owner of his real estate broker
business.
The law firm rents its two Napa offices from the tenancy in common, made up of
you and five of the law partners. Your law firm has a five-year lease for its current office
space with two renewables of five years each.
The firm leases its St. Helena office space. All of your real property interests
exceed $2,000 or involve a lease of over one-month in duration. Additionally, you and
your husband hold real property interests as community property. All of these real
property interests are worth over $2,000.
You specifically would like to know whether taking a leave of absence from your
firm or severing all ties with the law partnership would eliminate any potential economic
interests you may have in the firm or its clients.


File No. I-02-082
Page No. 4
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public ofiicial from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision.3 (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8) ) Based on the facts you have
provided, you are not yet a public offrcial.a Your questions concern the identification of
your economic interests, step three of the conflict-of-interest analysis. Therefore, we
provide you general guidance.
What are your economic interests?
Section 87103 provides lhat a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 5 of $2,000 or more (Section 871 03 (a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation I 8703. t (b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
3 This analysis is summarized in the Commission document entifled, "Can I Vote? Conflict-of-
Interest Overview" (enclosed).
' You state that your firm contracts with the City of St. Helena and the Town of Yountville for
city attorney services. Please note if you, pu$uant to a contract with the City of St. Helena or the Town of
Yountville, errgage in the actions specified in regulation 18701(a)(2), you may currently be a "public
offi.cial" for purposes of the Act. We have enclosed a copy of this regulation for your futme reference.
t An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official 's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public ofFrcial, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family'' is defined
at Section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


File No. I-02-082
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87 1 03 (e); Regulation 18703 .4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financiai
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
Law Firm
Because you are a partner in your law firm, you have an economic interest in this
business entity. Additionally, you have an economic interest in the firm on the basis of
the income you have received from it. "Income" means.
" . . .a payment received, including but not limited to any
salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds
from any sale, gift, including any gift of food or beverage,
loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by
the filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or
contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by
any person other than an employer, and including any
community property interest in the income of a spouse.
Income also includes an outstanding loan. Income of an
individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of
any business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a
l0-percent interest or greater. 'Income,' other than a gift,
does not include income received from any source outside
the jurisdiction and not doing business within the
jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the
jurisdiction, or not having done business within the
jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any
statement or other action is required under this title... ."
(Section 82030.)
You will have an economic interest in the firm any time that income is received or
promised to the firm in an amount of $500 or more from it attributable to you within 12
nronths prior to the time that a decision before you is made. Since the Act requires that
you aggregate payments received during the specified 12 months, taking a leave of
absence lrom the firm, whether selling your partnership interest or not, would not in itself
eliminate your economic interest in the firm as a source of income to you. You must look


File No I-02-082
Page No. 6
back 12 months to determine whether a person is a source of income to you. In other
words, income received or promised is disqualifying for 12 months after you received or
were promised the income.
Clients of
Law Firm
Pursuant to section 87103(c) and 82030, you also have an interest in an individual
who is a source of income to your firm since your ownership interest in the firm equals or
exceeds l}Yo. You have an interest of approximately 14Yo in your law firm. You will
have an economic interest in a particular client if the l4Yo income attributed to you from
the client will aggregate to $500 or more during the relevant 12 month time period. This
means that you will have an economic interest in any client who makes or promises a
payment ol$3,571 .43 or more.over the time period to the firm.6
As with the law firm, you must evaluate whether you have received income (or
income has been promised) from a particular client during the 12 month time frame prior
to the time the relevant decision is to be made; relinquishing your partnership interest
alone would not eliminate an economic interest in a client.
Law Firm's Lease
A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair market value.
(Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) An "interest in real property" includes:
"...any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property located in
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by
the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate
familyT if the fair market value of the interest is two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in real property
of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the
individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 1O-percent interest or greater." (Section
82033 .) (Footnote added.)
Theretbre, you also have a real property interest in property your law firm owns or leases
if your pro-rata share of the real property or leasehold is valued at $2,000 or more.
(Sections 82033 and 87103(b); Atigh Advice Letter, No. I-88-140.) Because you have a
greater than l0o/o interest in the law firm, you have an economic interest in your pro rata
(14%) share of the lease for the St. Helena office space leased by your law firm. If this
pro rata share amounts to $2,000 or more, you have areal property interest and, therefore,
an economic interest in this leased property.
6 This result occurs because $3,571.43 divided by 14% equals approximately $500.00
7 "Immediate family'' means the spouse and dependent children.


File No. I-02-082
Page No. 7
Office Buildings
Because you have a greater than l0o/o interest in the law firm, you have an
economic interest in your pro rata(14%) share of the law firm's ownership of these office
buildings. If this pro rata share amounts to $2,000 or more, you have areal property
interest and as a result, an economic interest in the buildings.
Rental of Office Space
The terms "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest" as used in section
82033 do not include the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less.
(Regulation 18233.) Therefore, you would not have an real property interest in office
space which you rented on a month to month basis, provided you do not receive a
discount and are paying the fair market rental value for the office space.
Personal Residence
Assuming your interest in your personal residence is $2,000 or more, you have an
economic interest in your personal residence.
Triplex
Assuming your interest in your tnplex is $2,000 or more, you have an economlc
interest in the triplex. Additionally, you will have an economic interest in any tenant, a
source of income, from whom you receive $500 or more in income during the 12 months
prior to the time a relevant decision is made.
Recreational Property
You will only have an economic interest in real property which is located in your
jurisdiction. Because the recreational property is located in Lake Tahoe, outside of the
Napa County jurisdiction, you do not have an economic interest in this property.
Real Estate Business
A public official can have an economic interest in a business entity if the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more in the business entity.
(Section 87 1 03 (a).) "Investment" means:
" ...any financial interest in or security issued by a
business entity, including but not limited to common stock,
preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt instruments
and any partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or
other filer, or his or her immediate family, if the business


File No. I-02-082
Page No. 8
entity or any parent, subsidiary or otherwise related
business entity has an interest in real property in the
jurisdiction, or does business or plans to do business in the
jurisdiction, or has done business within the jurisdiction at
any time during the two years prior to the time any
statement or other action is required under this title. No
asset shall be deemed an investment unless its fair market
value equals or exceeds two thousand dollars ($2,000)...."
(Section 82034.)
Consequently, if you or your husband have invested $2,000 or more in your husband's
real estate broker business, you have an economic interest in this business entity.
The second economic interest you may have in your husband's real estate broker
business may be derived from income you receive from this business. For the purposes
of the Act, "income" is defined in Section 82030(a) to include "any community property
interest in the income of a spouse." Any community property interest which you may
have in your husband's income from this business is considered your income under
section 82030 and may constitute a potentially disqualifying interest under Section
87103(c). If, however, as a result of a formal separate property agreement with your
husband, you have no community property interest in your husband's income, his income
cannot be the basis for a conflict of interest under Sections 871 00 and 87 103 . (Vassey
Advice Letter, A-86-20 1 .)
As mentioned, "income" is also defined in Section 82030 as "a pro rata share of
any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
directly, indirectly or benefici ally , a 1 0-percent interest or greater. " Accordingly, if your
husband owns a 10Yo or greater interest in his real estate broker business, the clients of
this business are considered sources of income to you based on a community property
interest in your husband's pro rata share of the gross receipts from those clients. While
this provision would generally make your husband's clients business sources of income
to you, the L}o/o pass-through rule would not apply to these facts due to a separate
property agreement as discussed above. (Vassey, suprd; Shaw Advice Letter, No. I-90-
377.)
While in previous letters we have advised that an offrcial does not have a
community property interest in the income of his or her spouse where the official and the
spouse have a separate property agreement eliminating such a community property
interest; ultimately, we are not finders of fact and cannot not opine as to the status of the
respective property rights of a couple where it remains a factual and legal determination
to be made in an arena outside of the Act. (See Perrott Advice Letler, No. I-00-141;
Alorales Advice Letter, No. A-99-2a6@); Vassey, supra.)
Nevertheless, you will still have an investment interest in your husband's business
since the definition of "investment" includes an investment interest held by your
husband, a member of your immediate family, even if held as separate property. (See


File No. l-02-082
Page No. 9
Fargo Advice Letter, No. I-91-089; Shaw, supra.) For this reason, devising a separate
property agreement will not eliminate your economic interest in his business. Also,
please note that you cannot reduce your real property interests through a separate
property agreement for the same reason; the definition of "interest in real property"
similarly includes an ownership interest held by a member of your immediate family.
(Section 82034.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
6
By
ocanegra
StaffCounsel,
Division
Enclosures

sections.question:
1. What relationship may you maintain with your law firm and still effectively eliminate
any source of income or other economic interest for you in the firm?
2. What relationship may you maintain with your law firm and eliminate any source of
income in the firm's clients?
3. When have you received income attributed to you from your law firm's clients?
4. Will your rental of offrce space on a month-to-month basis from the law firm create an
economic interest for you?
5. Could a conflict of interest arise if a partner or employee of the law firm represents a
client before the board?
' Oriy formal written advice confers the immunity provided under section 83114(b) and
regulation 18329(b)(7). Informal assistance does not provide immunity. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy
enclosed.)


File No. l-02-082
6. Could you eliminate your economic interest(s) in your husband's business through a
separate property agreement?
7. Can you reduce your real property interests by exchanging equal interests in real
property with your husband and then entering into a separate property agreement with
him?
sections.conclusion:
1. As a public official, you would have an economic interest in your firm as a source of
income to you if you receive $500 or more within 12 months preceding the time a
relevant decision is made. Additionally, you would have an economic interest in the firm
if you are a director, officer, partner, trustee, or employee of firm. If you meet this
criteria at the time a relevant decision is being made, you would be unable to eliminate
your economic interest in the firm.
2. Because you have an interest of approximalelyl4%o in your law firm, T4o/o of the
income received by the firm from each client will be attributed to you. You would have
an economic interest in a particular client if the I4Yo income attributed to you from the
client aggregates to $500 or more during the relevant l2 month time period. As with the
law firm, you must evaluate whether you have received income from a particular client
during the 12 month time frame prior to the time the relevant decision is to be made,
relinquishing your partnership interest alone will not eliminate an economic interest in a
client.
3. Because your income includes apro rata share of any income of the firm, you will
have "received" income from a client upon your law firm's receipt of the income.
4. Because the terms "interest in real property'' and "leasehold interest" do not include
the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less, you would not have a
real property interest in office space which you rented on a month to month basis,
provided that you do not receive a discount and are paying the fair market rental value for
the office space.
5. We are unable to answer this question without more information relating to a specific
governmental decision. However, please be aware that you will have a conflict of
interest in a governmental decision if the decision will have a material financial effect on
a client in whom you have an economic interest or another economic interest of yours,
including your law firm. (See enclosed document, "Can I Vote? Conflict-of-Interest
Overview.")
6. Since the definition of "investment" includes an ownership interest owned by a public
official or his or her immediate family (i.e., your husband and dependent children), you
could not eliminate your economic (investment) interest in the business entity owned by
your husband through a separate property agreement if you or he has invested $2,000 or
more in this business entity.
7. You may not eliminate your interest in real property held by your husband after
exchanging equal interests and entering into a separate property agreement since the
definition of "interest in real property" includes an ownership interest owned by your
immediate family.

the filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or
contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by
any person other than an employer, and including any
community property interest in the income of a spouse.

individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of
any business entity or trust in which the individual or

back 12 months to determine whether a person is a source of income to you. In other

As with the law firm, you must evaluate whether you have received income (or

alone would not eliminate an economic interest in a client.
Law Firm's Lease
A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair market value.
(Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) An "interest in real property" includes:
"...any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property located in
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by
the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate
familyT if the fair market value of the interest is two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in real property
of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the
individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 1O-percent interest or greater." (Section
82033 .) (Footnote added.)
Theretbre, you also have a real property interest in property your law firm owns or leases
if your pro-rata share of the real property or leasehold is valued at $2,000 or more.
(Sections 82033 and 87103(b); Atigh Advice Letter, No. I-88-140.) Because you have a
greater than l0o/o interest in the law firm, you have an economic interest in your pro rata
(14%) share of the lease for the St. Helena office space leased by your law firm. If this
pro rata share amounts to $2,000 or more, you have areal property interest and, therefore,
an economic interest in this leased property.
6 This result occurs because $3,571.43 divided by 14% equals approximately $500.00
7 "Immediate family'' means the spouse and dependent children.
Office Buildings
Because you have a greater than l0o/o interest in the law firm, you have an
economic interest in your pro rata(14%) share of the law firm's ownership of these office
buildings. If this pro rata share amounts to $2,000 or more, you have areal property
interest and as a result, an economic interest in the buildings.
Rental of Office Space
The terms "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest" as used in section
82033 do not include the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less.
(Regulation 18233.) Therefore, you would not have an real property interest in office
space which you rented on a month to month basis, provided you do not receive a
discount and are paying the fair market rental value for the office space.
Personal Residence
Assuming your interest in your personal residence is $2,000 or more, you have an
economic interest in your personal residence.
Triplex
Assuming your interest in your tnplex is $2,000 or more, you have an economlc
interest in the triplex. Additionally, you will have an economic interest in any tenant, a
source of income, from whom you receive $500 or more in income during the 12 months
prior to the time a relevant decision is made.
Recreational Property
You will only have an economic interest in real property which is located in your
jurisdiction. Because the recreational property is located in Lake Tahoe, outside of the
Napa County jurisdiction, you do not have an economic interest in this property.
Real Estate Business
A public official can have an economic interest in a business entity if the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more in the business entity.
(Section 87 1 03 (a).) "Investment" means:
" ...any financial interest in or security issued by a
business entity, including but not limited to common stock,
preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt instruments
and any partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or
other filer, or his or her immediate family, if the business
entity or any parent, subsidiary or otherwise related
business entity has an interest in real property in the
jurisdiction, or does business or plans to do business in the
jurisdiction, or has done business within the jurisdiction at
any time during the two years prior to the time any
statement or other action is required under this title. No
asset shall be deemed an investment unless its fair market
value equals or exceeds two thousand dollars ($2,000)...."
(Section 82034.)
Consequently, if you or your husband have invested $2,000 or more in your husband's
real estate broker business, you have an economic interest in this business entity.
The second economic interest you may have in your husband's real estate broker
business may be derived from income you receive from this business. For the purposes
of the Act, "income" is defined in Section 82030(a) to include "any community property
interest in the income of a spouse." Any community property interest which you may
have in your husband's income from this business is considered your income under
section 82030 and may constitute a potentially disqualifying interest under Section
87103(c). If, however, as a result of a formal separate property agreement with your
husband, you have no community property interest in your husband's income, his income
cannot be the basis for a conflict of interest under Sections 871 00 and 87 103 . (Vassey
Advice Letter, A-86-20 1 .)
As mentioned, "income" is also defined in Section 82030 as "a pro rata share of
any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
directly, indirectly or benefici ally , a 1 0-percent interest or greater. " Accordingly, if your
husband owns a 10Yo or greater interest in his real estate broker business, the clients of
this business are considered sources of income to you based on a community property
interest in your husband's pro rata share of the gross receipts from those clients. While
this provision would generally make your husband's clients business sources of income
to you, the L}o/o pass-through rule would not apply to these facts due to a separate
property agreement as discussed above. (Vassey, suprd; Shaw Advice Letter, No. I-90-
377.)
While in previous letters we have advised that an offrcial does not have a
community property interest in the income of his or her spouse where the official and the
spouse have a separate property agreement eliminating such a community property
interest; ultimately, we are not finders of fact and cannot not opine as to the status of the
respective property rights of a couple where it remains a factual and legal determination
to be made in an arena outside of the Act. (See Perrott Advice Letler, No. I-00-141;
Alorales Advice Letter, No. A-99-2a6@); Vassey, supra.)
Nevertheless, you will still have an investment interest in your husband's business
since the definition of "investment" includes an investment interest held by your
husband, a member of your immediate family, even if held as separate property. (See


File No. l-02-082
Fargo Advice Letter, No. I-91-089; Shaw, supra.) For this reason, devising a separate
property agreement will not eliminate your economic interest in his business. Also,
please note that you cannot reduce your real property interests through a separate
property agreement for the same reason; the definition of "interest in real property"
similarly includes an ownership interest held by a member of your immediate family.
(Section 82034.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. What relationship may you maintain with your law firm and still effectively eliminate
any source of income or other economic interest for you in the firm?
2. What relationship may you maintain with your law firm and eliminate any source of
income in the firm's clients?
3. When have you received income attributed to you from your law firm's clients?
4. Will your rental of offrce space on a month-to-month basis from the law firm create an
economic interest for you?
5. Could a conflict of interest arise if a partner or employee of the law firm represents a
client before the board?
' Oriy formal written advice confers the immunity provided under section 83114(b) and
regulation 18329(b)(7). Informal assistance does not provide immunity. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy
enclosed.)


File No. l-02-082
6. Could you eliminate your economic interest(s) in your husband's business through a
separate property agreement?
7. Can you reduce your real property interests by exchanging equal interests in real
property with your husband and then entering into a separate property agreement with
him?

CONCLUSION: 1. As a public official, you would have an economic interest in your firm as a source of
income to you if you receive $500 or more within 12 months preceding the time a
relevant decision is made. Additionally, you would have an economic interest in the firm
if you are a director, officer, partner, trustee, or employee of firm. If you meet this
criteria at the time a relevant decision is being made, you would be unable to eliminate
your economic interest in the firm.
2. Because you have an interest of approximalelyl4%o in your law firm, T4o/o of the
income received by the firm from each client will be attributed to you. You would have
an economic interest in a particular client if the I4Yo income attributed to you from the
client aggregates to $500 or more during the relevant l2 month time period. As with the
law firm, you must evaluate whether you have received income from a particular client
during the 12 month time frame prior to the time the relevant decision is to be made,
relinquishing your partnership interest alone will not eliminate an economic interest in a
client.
3. Because your income includes apro rata share of any income of the firm, you will
have "received" income from a client upon your law firm's receipt of the income.
4. Because the terms "interest in real property'' and "leasehold interest" do not include
the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month or less, you would not have a
real property interest in office space which you rented on a month to month basis,
provided that you do not receive a discount and are paying the fair market rental value for
the office space.
5. We are unable to answer this question without more information relating to a specific
governmental decision. However, please be aware that you will have a conflict of
interest in a governmental decision if the decision will have a material financial effect on
a client in whom you have an economic interest or another economic interest of yours,
including your law firm. (See enclosed document, "Can I Vote? Conflict-of-Interest
Overview.")
6. Since the definition of "investment" includes an ownership interest owned by a public
official or his or her immediate family (i.e., your husband and dependent children), you
could not eliminate your economic (investment) interest in the business entity owned by
your husband through a separate property agreement if you or he has invested $2,000 or
more in this business entity.
7. You may not eliminate your interest in real property held by your husband after
exchanging equal interests and entering into a separate property agreement since the
definition of "interest in real property" includes an ownership interest owned by your
immediate family.
